title: Air New Zealand Shares At New Low: Fuel Crisis Forces Guidance Withdrawal - The Bull
description:
Fuel Shock Triggers Strategic Reset Within the past week, Air New Zealand took the unusual step of suspending its FY2026 earnings guidance, explicitly citing unprecedented volatility in global jet fuel markets.
Air New Zealand had been banking on a volume recovery and network normalisation as core pillars of its post-pandemic earnings recovery.
However, the scale and speed of the fuel market dislocation has overwhelmed those efforts.
The fuel crisis also comes at a time when Air New Zealand is navigating longer-term environmental and regulatory pressures.
The path forward for Air New Zealand shares hinge on factors largely outside management’s control: the trajectory of jet fuel prices, the duration of geopolitical tensions, and the willingness of consumers to absorb higher fares.
text:
Air New Zealand shares (NZE:AIR) slumped to a new 52-week low of NZ$0.44 before closing at NZ$0.45 into the weekend, extending year-to-date losses to 23.73 percent, after the carrier suspended its full-year earnings guidance amid an unprecedented surge in jet fuel prices.

The national carrier’s stock touched NZ$0.44 in early trading on the New Zealand Exchange, marking its lowest level since the COVID-19 recovery began. The shares closed down sharply at NZ$0.45, with trading volumes elevated as markets digested the implications of the guidance withdrawal. The sell-off reflects mounting concern that the airline’s fragile return to profitability has been derailed by external cost pressures beyond management’s control. Air New Zealand’s market capitalisation has now fallen below levels seen during some of the darkest days of the pandemic recovery, despite strong underlying travel demand across the Tasman and domestic networks.

Fuel Shock Triggers Strategic Reset

Within the past week, Air New Zealand took the unusual step of suspending its FY2026 earnings guidance, explicitly citing unprecedented volatility in global jet fuel markets. The airline had anchored its previous outlook on jet fuel trading around US$85–90 per barrel, but recent market moves have seen prices spike toward US$150–200 per barrel as geopolitical tensions disrupt refining capacity and widen the crack spread between crude oil and refined jet fuel products.

The guidance withdrawal follows an already-disappointing first-half FY2026 result, in which the airline reported a net loss of approximately NZ$59 million. Management had previously indicated that the second half would deliver earnings roughly in line with or slightly below the first half, assuming stable fuel costs. That scenario is no longer operative. The airline’s hedging strategy, which covers roughly 80 percent of its Brent crude exposure for the remainder of FY2026, has provided only partial protection because the extreme widening of refining margins means actual jet fuel costs have surged well beyond hedged crude prices.

In response, Air New Zealand has announced it will cancel approximately 1,100 flights between now and early May, affecting an estimated 44,000 passengers. The capacity cuts are a tactical measure to avoid operating loss-making routes at elevated fuel costs, with management prioritising yield over volume. Fare increases have already been implemented across the network, and further pricing actions are under consideration. The airline has also flagged potential adjustments to its network and schedule, including possible reductions on fuel-inefficient or lower-margin routes.

Operational Pressures Mount

The operational impact of the fuel crisis extends beyond immediate cost pressures. Air New Zealand had been banking on a volume recovery and network normalisation as core pillars of its post-pandemic earnings recovery. Cutting more than a thousand flights at a time of strong underlying travel demand signals that the fuel shock is large enough to render some routes uneconomic, even with robust passenger loads.

The airline’s FY2025 financial year had seen a return to profitability, with net profit after tax of approximately NZ$126 million, supported by strong premium and corporate demand, cost transformation initiatives that delivered roughly NZ$100 million in savings, and improved operational performance. That progress now appears at risk. The swing from profit back to loss in just one year, even before the latest fuel surge is fully reflected in results, has forced markets to reassess the durability of the carrier’s earnings power.

Air New Zealand’s cost-out programme, known internally as the Kia Mau transformation, had been a key component of management’s strategy to offset inflationary pressures and rebuild margins. However, the scale and speed of the fuel market dislocation has overwhelmed those efforts. The airline’s relatively high exposure to domestic and regional routes, while a structural competitive advantage in normal times, becomes a liability when fuel costs spike because shorter-haul operations burn proportionally more fuel per revenue kilometre.

Industry-Wide Headwinds

Air New Zealand is among the first Asia-Pacific airlines to formally highlight operating cost pressures from the ongoing conflict-driven spike in fuel prices, but it is far from alone in facing these headwinds. Airlines globally are warning of margin pressure, particularly on long-haul and fuel-intensive routes. The carrier also faces competitive intensity in the Australasian market, where Qantas, Virgin Australia, and Air New Zealand have been vying for share in what some analysts have characterised as a price war.

New Zealand domestic airfares had already risen sharply over the past few years, with consumer advocacy groups highlighting increases of up to 300 percent on some routes. The airline now faces a delicate balancing act: raising fares further to offset fuel costs risks dampening price-sensitive leisure demand, while holding prices steady would deepen losses. Management’s decision to cut capacity suggests a belief that yield management and network discipline offer a better path through the crisis than chasing market share at any cost.

The fuel crisis also comes at a time when Air New Zealand is navigating longer-term environmental and regulatory pressures. Approximately 18 months ago, the airline dropped its 2030 climate goal, citing delays in delivery of fuel-efficient aircraft and the cost and availability of sustainable aviation fuel. That decision drew criticism from ESG-focused investors and environmental groups, and may have narrowed the pool of capital willing to own the stock. The airline is caught between climate policy expectations and the economic reality of its fleet and fuel mix, a tension that is likely to persist for years.

Outlook

The suspension of earnings guidance leaves markets with limited visibility on Air New Zealand’s near-term profitability. Bulls argue that the airline’s quasi-monopoly on many regional domestic routes, strong brand recognition in New Zealand, and leverage to international tourism inflows position it to recover sharply once macro shocks fade. The carrier’s structural importance to New Zealand’s NZ$17 billion-plus international tourism industry provides a degree of strategic resilience, and the government remains a major shareholder, offering an implicit backstop in a severe downturn.

Bears counter that the recent swing from profit back to loss demonstrates the business has limited margin of safety against external shocks. The risk of further capital raisings or government interventions is non-trivial if conditions worsen. The airline’s high operating leverage means that even modest changes in fuel costs or demand can produce large swings in earnings, and the current environment offers little clarity on when fuel markets might normalise. Technical analysts note that the stock is trading near multi-year lows with limited near-term support levels, suggesting further downside is possible if fuel remains elevated or if second-half losses exceed market expectations.

Some market participants see today’s share price as embedding panic pricing on a transient fuel shock, particularly if geopolitical tensions ease and refining capacity returns to normal. Others view the sell-off as a rational repricing of a highly cyclical, capital-intensive business with limited pricing power in a cost-shock environment. The next catalyst will likely be management’s update on second-half trading and any revised framework for thinking about full-year earnings, expected in the coming weeks.

The path forward for Air New Zealand shares hinge on factors largely outside management’s control: the trajectory of jet fuel prices, the duration of geopolitical tensions, and the willingness of consumers to absorb higher fares. In the meantime, the airline’s focus on capital discipline, yield management, and cost control offers a defensive posture but does little to inspire confidence in a near-term earnings recovery.

text:
Highlights

Firepoint Energy has engaged Ardour Capital Investments as financial advisor to support capital formation as it advances its waste coal repurposing platform for critical minerals recovery.

The company is developing a zero-emission plasma gasification process that converts legacy waste coal into critical minerals and sustainable aviation fuel without incineration.

Firepoint’s technology addresses U.S. critical mineral supply vulnerabilities identified by the Department of Defense as a strategic national security priority.

Firepoint Energy Inc (opens in a new tab)., a Pennsylvania-based critical minerals recovery company, announced it has engaged Ardour Capital Investments, LLC (opens in a new tab) as financial advisor to support capital formation and strategic growth as the company advances its waste coal repurposing platform.

NewYork–based Ardour Capital brings more than 25 years of experience advising clean energy and climate technology companies. Under the agreement, the firm will assist Firepoint Energy with financing strategy and strategic financial initiatives as the company moves toward commercial deployment of its proprietary technology.

Firepoint Energy is developing a zero-emission plasma gasification process that converts legacy waste coal into critical minerals and sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) without incineration. The company’s pilot infrastructure is nearing completion, and a formal research and industry collaboration with Penn State University has recently been established.

“As we move into a capital-intensive phase of development, Ardour Capital’s expertise in alternative energy and climate-positive technologies will be instrumental,” said Bill Smith, Chief Executive Officer of Firepoint Energy. The company’s next phase includes manufacturing plasma gasifiers for waste coal processing and identifying additional sites with significant waste coal inventories.

Firepoint believes its technology could help address U.S. supply vulnerabilities in critical minerals, which the Department of Defense has identified as a strategic national security priority. Can this approach help lead to critical mineral resilience in America?

For more information visit firepoint.energy and ardourcapital.com.

text:
The Senate Agriculture Committee held its first in what will be a series of hearings on how lawmakers can support farmers and build up the U.S. farm economy.

The March 10 hearing, “Increasing Domestic Consumption of U.S.-Grown Agricultural Products,” gathered recommendations from agriculture industry leaders on policy improvements to help boost domestic consumption.

With farm bankruptcies rising, committee Chairman John Boozman’s (R-Ark.) opening statement underscored the dire circumstances, saying this is “a time of great financial stress for America’s farmers. If you are putting something in the ground, you are losing money. It doesn’t matter what you are planting or where you are planting it, no row crop is currently profitable.”

While President Trump has prioritized trade deals as a way to expand markets for agricultural products, Boozman noted China has proved an unreliable trading partner, and other countries like Brazil are moving in to challenge America’s leadership in global export markets.

This dynamic was the impetus for looking at ways to bolster opportunities for producers at home and to “think about how we can build more robust domestic markets that will not subject our producers to the whims of foreign governments,” Boozman said. “This hearing is intended to begin that conversation.”

He said there will be discussions with processors, manufacturers and retailers on topics pertaining to domestic market development such as impediments, incentives, processing infrastructure and legal, regulatory or procedural obstacles.

In her opening statement at the committee hearing, Ranking Member Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.) emphasized the need to create “innovative sources of revenue” for farmers enduring a challenging farm economy.

American farmers need more home-based markets for their home-grown products. “They need it now, more than ever,” Klobuchar said. An example is allowing year-round sales of E15 ethanol fuel blends, she suggested, as well as increasing production of sustainable aviation fuel.

“We can also expand domestic opportunities by investing in local markets, by purchasing fresh produce and meat from small and midsized farmers,” she added. “We can support local supply chains and families. ... We can also invest in organic agriculture, expand support for rural businesses and invest in research to develop new revenue streams for farmers.”

Need for 'durable demand'

Zippy Duvall, president of the American Farm Bureau Federation, which represents more than 5 million farmer members, testified at the hearing, thanking Congress and the Administration for their “investments in risk management, trade promotion, conservation and addressing animal diseases,” as well as the recent bridge payments to row-crop farmers.

“But farmers do not want to rely on government aid. And aid should not be needed year after year,” Duvall said. “That’s not normal volatility; it’s structural imbalance. And that’s why this hearing is so important. We must strengthen domestic demand for American agricultural products. We must reinforce our production capacity of critical ag supplies and restore domestic processing capacity.”

Also testifying at the hearing were American Soybean Association president Scott Metzger, North Dakota Farmers Union president Matt Perdue, National Corn Growers Association president Jed Bower, International Fresh Produce Association chief executive officer Cathy Burns and National Cotton Council chairman Nathan Reed.

Several themes emerged from their testimony on potential solutions. Suggestions included:

Although the hearing focused on domestic market opportunities, witnesses also acknowledged the importance of continuing to build and expand export markets, such as with market development programs and by reducing trade barriers through new and existing trade deals.

“American agriculture remains one of the most innovative and productive sectors in the world. The opportunity before us is to ensure that markets, infrastructure and policy evolve alongside that productivity,” Duvall said. “Demand must grow in durable ways. Processing capacity must be modern, regionally resilient and economically viable. Trade relationships must be enforceable and reciprocal. And the farmland that underpins this system must remain economically viable for the next generation.”

title: Norwegian Air Shuttle Debuts Denmark's First SAF-Powered Domestic Flight Route | ittn.ie
authors: Stephen Aherne, Geoff Percival, Jack Goddard, Allie Sheehan, Prerna Shah
description:
Norwegian Air Shuttle has formally started flying Denmark’s fist domestic flight route powered by sustainable aviation fuel (SAF).
The route – between Copenhagen and Aalborg – will be operated by planes with 40% SAF powering them, and forms part of Denmark’s national green aviation strategy aimed at cutting aviation carbon emissions.
The launch of the service comes as SAF hits a crossroads – the EU pushing for increased SAF use around European airports, but with current demand far outweighing small supply levels.

title: X‑76 aircraft moves into build phase as Bell clears critical design review
description:
Bell Textron has successfully held the Critical Design Review (CDR) for the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)’s SPeed and Runway INdependent Technologies (SPRINT) programme.
In Phase 1A and 1B, Bell completed conceptual and preliminary design efforts for the SPRINT X-plane.
Phase 2 includes detailed design, build, and ground testing culminating in flight test during Phase 3.
The SPRINT programme brings together all of this into one vehicle to provide runway independence with jet speeds.
“The Bell team looks forward to seeing X-76 take flight and bring this capability to our warfighters,” added Hurst.
text:
Bell Textron has successfully held the Critical Design Review (CDR) for the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA)’s SPeed and Runway INdependent Technologies (SPRINT) programme. This milestone allows Bell to begin building the next-generation aircraft demonstrator, recently designated as the X-76.

“Bell is honored to receive the X-76 designation and continue the spirit of American innovation honoring the founding of the United States in 1776,” said Jason Hurst, Bell SVP, Engineering. “This is an important milestone as the Bell and DARPA team advances to a historic first in aviation history and fulfill our mission of developing next-generation vertical-lift aircraft.”

In July 2025, Bell announced the company was down-selected for Phase 2 of the programme in the latest chapter of its 90-year history of X-plane development.

The goal of the SPRINT programme, jointly funded by DARPA and U.S. Special Operations Command, is to advance next generation runway independent technologies that can be scaled to different military aircraft through designing an aircraft with the ability to cruise at speeds from 400 to 450 knots at relevant altitudes and hover in austere environments from unprepared surfaces.

In Phase 1A and 1B, Bell completed conceptual and preliminary design efforts for the SPRINT X-plane. Phase 2 includes detailed design, build, and ground testing culminating in flight test during Phase 3.

Since its inception, Bell has pushed the known boundaries of flight through pioneering high speed and vertical lift aircraft, from the X-1 to the XV-3 and XV-15. The SPRINT programme brings together all of this into one vehicle to provide runway independence with jet speeds.

“The Bell team looks forward to seeing X-76 take flight and bring this capability to our warfighters,” added Hurst.

title: PhD Defence by Hamza Abid - Aalborg Universitet
description:
The PhD thesis investigates the potential to mitigate Europe's dependence on imported fossil fuels while addressing the climate crisis through harnessing renewable energy from the North Sea.
Specifically, the research assesses the potential for integrating large-scale offshore wind and renewable hydrogen production into the broader energy system, with a detailed feasibility study focused on the Danish North Sea energy hubs.
The thesis delivers a comprehensive socio-economic and techno-economic analysis, addressing key challenges in energy system integration, decarbonisation pathways, and energy security, all framed within Europe's evolving geopolitical landscape.
A systematic literature review quantifies the role of hydrogen in future decarbonised European energy system.
Utilising systems modelling and grounded in choice awareness, the thesis presents different pathways for hydrogen and hydrogen derived sustainable aviation fuel production.
text:
The PhD thesis investigates the potential to mitigate Europe's dependence on imported fossil fuels while addressing the climate crisis through harnessing renewable energy from the North Sea. Specifically, the research assesses the potential for integrating large-scale offshore wind and renewable hydrogen production into the broader energy system, with a detailed feasibility study focused on the Danish North Sea energy hubs. The thesis delivers a comprehensive socio-economic and techno-economic analysis, addressing key challenges in energy system integration, decarbonisation pathways, and energy security, all framed within Europe's evolving geopolitical landscape.

A systematic literature review quantifies the role of hydrogen in future decarbonised European energy system. Utilising systems modelling and grounded in choice awareness, the thesis presents different pathways for hydrogen and hydrogen derived sustainable aviation fuel production. By comparing stand alone and system level perspectives, the thesis also presents different hydrogen export strategies from offshore energy hubs balancing economic competitiveness and strategic autonomy.

title: Will the Carbon-Aviation Fuel Breakthrough Support Its High-Risk Valuation? | Bitget News
description:
However, the company’s business model is currently experiencing significant strain, prompting a thorough reevaluation of its future.
The market has responded decisively: the company’s market capitalization has plummeted to $3.96 million, a drop of more than 98% from its previous highs.
This dramatic decline reflects a near-total loss of investor faith in the company’s current approach.
Additionally, Helena Partners, another major creditor, agreed to release cash collateral to help repay around $1.1 million in debt.
Valuation and Assessing Margin of Safety DevvStream’s financial metrics tell a story of contrasts.
text:
DevvStream Corp: A Pure-Play Model Under Pressure

DevvStream Corp exemplifies a company built around a focused business strategy now facing a critical test of its underlying worth. The firm, which is dedicated to monetizing carbon and renewable energy assets, was founded on the belief that environmental responsibility and financial success could go hand in hand. Its core objective has been to develop and market environmental assets, establishing itself as a key player in the expanding carbon markets. However, the company’s business model is currently experiencing significant strain, prompting a thorough reevaluation of its future.

Financial Struggles and Market Reaction

The company’s recent financial performance highlights the gravity of its situation. In the latest fiscal year, DevvStream reported revenues of only $26,000 against a net loss of $12.1 million, resulting in a staggering negative profit margin exceeding -46,000%. These figures point to an unsustainable rate of cash outflow. Financial indicators are weak, with a Piotroski F-Score of just 3 out of 9, and operating cash flow of -$6.4 million falling well short of net income. The market has responded decisively: the company’s market capitalization has plummeted to $3.96 million, a drop of more than 98% from its previous highs. This dramatic decline reflects a near-total loss of investor faith in the company’s current approach.

Value Investing Dilemma

For those who focus on value investing, DevvStream’s predicament raises a fundamental issue. When a company’s revenues are minuscule compared to its market value, conventional measures of profitability and growth lose relevance. The focus shifts to assessing intrinsic value and ensuring a margin of safety. The current share price suggests the market has deeply discounted the company’s mission and past valuations. The key question is whether this discount provides enough protection against permanent capital loss, or if it simply mirrors the market’s realistic view of a failing business model. While the company’s distress is evident, the route to potential value—whether through recovery, restructuring, or winding down—remains uncertain.

Restructuring Efforts: Debt Reduction and Financial Relief

DevvStream has recently taken decisive action to address its liquidity crisis by reducing its debt burden. Through a series of transactions finalized this week, DevvStream has cut its outstanding debt by about $5.9 million. The process involved major creditors converting their claims into equity, effectively exchanging debt for company shares. Notably, Focus Impact Partners converted all of its 5.3% convertible notes and approximately $5.5 million in consulting fees into equity, with the conversion price set at a 12.9% premium over the March 10 share price—signaling confidence from a key partner.

This restructuring has had a measurable impact on the company’s balance sheet, improving its trailing twelve-month current ratio from 0.12. Although still far from ideal, this marks a significant step away from the brink of insolvency.

Additionally, Helena Partners, another major creditor, agreed to release cash collateral to help repay around $1.1 million in debt. In return, Helena provided a $700,000 interest-free loan to bolster working capital, with interest payments deferred until May 2026.

For value investors, the premium conversion is a mixed signal. While it demonstrates that some partners believe in the company’s long-term prospects, it also results in shareholder dilution. The immediate financial relief is tangible, but the underlying business challenges remain. Revenue is still minimal, and the market cap continues to reflect skepticism. The restructuring offers temporary stability and a better capital structure, but a sustainable path to profitability has yet to emerge. The margin of safety now depends on the company’s ability to successfully implement new strategic initiatives with its improved financial footing.

Seeking Intrinsic Value: New Ventures and Market Position

DevvStream’s future now rests on a bold new initiative: a planned merger with Southern Energy Renewables. The aim is to establish a platform focused on producing affordable, carbon-negative sustainable aviation fuel. This represents a strategic shift from negligible revenues toward a potentially scalable, profitable business. The critical question for investors is whether this new direction can create a lasting competitive advantage in the unpredictable carbon markets.

The success of this venture hinges on DevvStream’s expertise in generating cash flow from environmental asset sales. The business model involves managing portfolios of carbon credits and renewable energy certificates, as well as offering project development services. This creates two main revenue streams: direct sales of verified credits and recurring project management fees. However, the stability of this advantage is uncertain, given the volatility of environmental credit markets, which are influenced by regulatory changes, technological advancements, and global demand. The company’s track record offers little comfort—recent operating cash flow of -$6.4 million was less than half its net loss, indicating that past profits were not supported by real cash generation. Any future success must be rooted in genuine, sustainable cash flow from the new fuel platform.

The merger with Southern Energy Renewables is intended to provide the scale and technical know-how needed to execute this vision. It’s a classic strategy of pairing a struggling company with a promising operational business. Yet, the market’s current valuation of $3.96 million reflects deep skepticism, shaped by DevvStream’s history of weak cash flow and the speculative nature of the new venture. While the restructuring offers some financial breathing room, it does not eliminate the fundamental risks. The margin of safety will ultimately depend on the company’s ability to shift from burning cash to consistently monetizing its environmental assets. The catalyst is clear, but the journey to realizing intrinsic value is likely to be long and uncertain.

Valuation and Assessing Margin of Safety

DevvStream’s financial metrics tell a story of contrasts. Its market capitalization of $3.96 million reflects the market’s assessment of its current, nearly dormant operations. However, its enterprise value stands at $16.27 million, highlighting the significant debt that the recent restructuring has started to address. This gap is central to any calculation of margin of safety. Any potential safety lies not in the existing business, but in the possible value of the new platform under development.

For value investors, the margin of safety is the cushion between intrinsic value and market price. In DevvStream’s case, intrinsic value is highly speculative and depends entirely on the successful execution of the merger with Southern Energy Renewables. The current share price represents a steep discount to enterprise value, but this is largely due to the company’s debt load and the high risk of failure. The recent debt reduction of $5.9 million strengthens the capital structure and extends the company’s runway, but it does not create value on its own—it simply reduces the risk of immediate collapse as the company attempts to build a new business.

Key factors to monitor include the successful integration of the merger, the generation of meaningful cash flow from the new venture, and the direction of carbon credit prices. The company’s history of poor earnings quality means that future profitability must be grounded in robust, sustainable cash generation. The volatility of carbon markets remains a significant risk, even as they are central to the company’s new strategy.

Ultimately, DevvStream’s situation is marked by considerable uncertainty. While the current valuation offers a substantial discount, realizing any intrinsic value will require patience and disciplined execution. The margin of safety is not found in the current numbers, but in the willingness to wait for the company’s new initiatives to bear fruit. For those willing to take the risk, this may be a classic value trap: the price is low, but the company must first prove it can create lasting value.

text:
Southern Energy Renewables Announces $1.4 Billion Biofuels Facility in Louisiana

Southern Energy Renewables has announced a significant investment to develop a production facility for green methanol and sustainable aviation fuel, according to a report from Hydrogen Central. The project, located in St. Charles Parish, Louisiana, will involve a capital expenditure of $1.4 billion.

The facility is designed to process wood-waste biomass from the region into fuels characterized by low lifecycle carbon emissions. The company anticipates the creation of 120 direct new jobs at the site, with an average annual salary noted as being above the local parish average. Broader economic projections estimate the development could lead to over 500 total new job opportunities when considering indirect employment.

State officials highlighted the project's alignment with Louisiana's existing energy infrastructure and workforce. The development is described as the company's first commercial-scale operation within the state. Pre-construction planning and site work are currently in progress, with the construction phase slated to commence in the latter part of 2027. The facility is expected to become operational by the end of 2029.

To secure the project, Louisiana extended an incentives package to the developer. This package includes a performance-based grant for infrastructure and access to a workforce development program. The company is also anticipated to utilize a state industrial tax exemption.

The President of the United States is not mentioned in the source material in relation to this project.

title: Neste announces changes to leadership team and organization structure
description:
Neste, the world’s leading producer of renewable diesel and sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), has appointed new leadership team members as part of an organizational change that primarily affects the company’s Renewable Products business.
As of April 1, 2026, Renewable Products commercial, refining, and feedstock sourcing and trading will be represented in Neste’s leadership team.
At the same time, Neste will establish a Renewable Products, North America business unit, responsible for all renewable products business in North America, including regional feedstock and commercial operations as well as Martinez joint venture.
From 1 April 2026 onwards, Neste’s leadership team members will consist of Heikki Malinen, president and CEO, and the following leadership team members, who will all report to the president and CEO: Jori Sahlsten, executive vice president, Oil Products business area Artturi Mikkola, senior vice president, Renewable Products Feedstock Sourcing and Trading Jukka Kanerva, senior vice president, Renewable Products Refining Carl Nyberg, senior vice president, Renewable Products Commercial N.N., president, Renewable Products, North America (Carl Nyberg as interim) Markku Korvenranta, executive vice president and chief operating officer Eeva Sipilä, chief financial officer (CFO) Hannele Jakosuo-Jansson, executive vice president, People & Culture

text:
HOUSTON, TX: Abundia Global Impact Group, Inc. (NYSE:AGIG) (“Abundia” or the “Company”), a low-carbon energy solutions company focused on converting biomass and plastics waste into high-value low-carbon fuels, today announced that it has entered a multi-project licensing agreement with Topsoe, a leading global provider of technology and solutions for the energy transition, to deploy its HydroFlex® technology for the upgrading process at Abundia’s first waste-plastics-to-renewable products recycling facility in Baytown, TX, the company stated in a press release.

The agreement includes a long-term commitment to deploy HydroFlex® across multiple future facilities and represents a core component of Abundia’s 2026 development plan toward final investment decision. The selection of Topsoe strengthens Abundia’s current plastics waste commercialization pathway and underscores its strategic focus to integrate high-quality, reliable, commercially validated technologies at scale across its multi-feedstock conversion and refining platform.

“Guided by the technical expertise of our engineering partner Nexus PMG, we are confident in the selection of Topsoe’s proven commercial-scale upgrading process,” said Ed Gillespie, Chief Executive Officer of Abundia. “HydroFlex® is recognized globally for its industry leading performance, reliability, and ability to produce high quality renewable fuels and chemicals that meet or exceed international specifications. The inclusion of Topsoe’s upgrade solution to our technology stack meaningfully de-risks our development path, reinforces the bankability of our technology configuration, and supports disciplined execution of our 2026 commercialization plan.”

“Topsoe’s technology and solutions are enabling the conversion of liquified plastic waste to fuels. This exciting project will significantly increase the production of Renewable Diesel & SAF in the U.S.,” said Henrik Rasmussen, Managing Director of Topsoe Americas. “We’re thrilled that Abundia has chosen Topsoe’s HydroFlex® technology for this milestone project and we’re excited to see the project become operational.”

A critical component of Abundia’s low carbon renewables production strategy is the integration of the front-end conversion technology through its partnership with Alterra and the back-end upgrading capability provided by Topsoe’s HydroFlex®. The synergies of this technology configuration support Abundia’s distinct vertically integrated waste-to-value model. These technology partnerships are at the foundation of the business strategy by positioning the Company to produce commercial grade “drop-in” renewable fuels and chemicals with consistent yield that meets the highest global regulatory performance standards.

Abundia – Topsoe Collaboration Highlights

Long term technology alignment supports scale development:

A multi-project license to operate Topsoe’s upgrading technology reinforces Abundia’s operating strategy to deliver economic value across stakeholders.

Strengthened competitive positioning:

Establishes a regional industry moat based on territory exclusivity terms which help to protect Abundia’s integrated technology solution and supports defensibility of its regional development pipeline and overall market position.

Independent technical validation:

Infrastructure engineering firm Nexus PMG conducted technical diligence supporting the selection of Topsoe’s upgrading platform as a capital-efficient and regulatory-ready pathway toward commercialization.

text:
The eMobility MOW 2026 forum in Malaga over the past two days has ended with an idea that experts, institutions and manufacturers share: the decarbonisation of transport cannot only involve electric cars.

The event at the Palacio de Ferias y Congresos brought together more than 5,000 professionals and 200 companies to discuss the future of transport. They all agreed that the most successful model involves a variety of vehicles: from full-on electric vehicles to those that use hydrogen and sustainable fuels.

Meetings in five auditoriums focused on how to adapt the industry to the present and the future. A total of 376 experts from all over the world took part in debates on the future of the automotive industry, the air, rail and maritime industries, urban transport and logistics.

The objective is clear: pursue decarbonisation and get as close as possible to zero emissions, but remembering that the future is not purely electric. The future is multi-energy and the market must adopt new technologies in an organic way, without forgetting the fundamental right of free movement and people's economic capacities.

Head of the International Automobile Federation (FIA) Carmelo Sanz de Barros said that there is still room for other technologies on Spain's roads: "Reducing the debate to a single technology impoverishes the transition, while diversification strengthens it. Technological neutrality is not a defence of the past, it is a guarantee for the future, guaranteeing European competitiveness."

The challenges facing transport are diverse. In aviation, for example, electric engines do not have the same place as in land vehicles. Instead, the sector is focusing on the application of artificial intelligence to optimise systems and on promoting sustainable aviation fuel, better known as SAF.

"AI has the capacity to analyse terabytes of data to find optimal performance," Valencia University Professor Pedro Manuel Quintero, said.

While some sectors and professionals view the 2030 agenda with suspicion, for Deputy Director of Transport & Environment Ioan Bucuras maintaining this goal is essential and strategic. "We have seen companies abandoning the agenda, but this discourages progress and investment," he said.

On land, the electric vehicle as the dominant technology is having a slower than expected transition. In 2025, it exceeded 100,000 registrations in Spain for the first time. However, infrastructure keeps lagging behind automotive developments.

"Of the 50,000 chargers in Spain, 20 per cent do not work. Of the remaining 80 per cent, 60 per cent are in cities, which is where they are least needed," Product Development Manager for Ayvens Salvador López said.

The outlook for motorcycles is even worse. While registrations of combustion engine motorcycles continue rising year after year, surpassing pre-pandemic levels, electric motorcycles have stagnated in recent years, with a negligible percentage of registrations (254,183 combustion engine motorcycles registered in 2025 compared to 11,037 electric ones).

For General Secretary of Anesdor José María Riaño, aid for the purchase of an electric vehicle discriminates against motorcyclists. "For an electric motorbike to be eligible for aid, it must cost less than 10,000 euros before taxes. This leaves a huge number of motorbikes out of the picture. In addition, those considered mopeds are not eligible for subsidies either," he said.

Another obstacle lies with charging stations: many of them do not have sockets compatible with motorcycle charging.

These challenges affect not only the type of vehicle but also its intended use. Accounts Deputy President at Scoobic Juanma Rufino stated that vans don't fit into the new urban landscape.

"We've been wasting our time for the last three years. If we were waiting for a gun to our head, now we have it. Vans and conventional vehicles have no place in this new urban concept," he said.

One of the major announcements of the Andalusian regional government during the MOW transport conference was the Renove plan, which will allow self-employed workers to give their old car or van for scrap and receive 3,000 euros to buy a new low-emission vehicle.

The Renove initiative involves the scrapping of cars that are at least ten years old. Applicants' new vehicles must have emissions of 120 grams of CO2 per kilometre or less. In addition, their price should not exceed 35,000 euros plus IVA tax.

Acquisition can be through direct purchase, leasing or renting. The biggest advantage is that the 3,000-euro discount is a direct benefit, with 100 per cent of the aid paid upfront upon approval. The regional government has earmarked five million euros of its own funds for the Renove plan.

In addition to the dozens of talks that took place, numerous companies took advantage of the event to showcase some of the latest innovations in the sector.

Dhamma Blue, for example, positioned an eight-metre-long boat in the middle of the esplanade. The main innovation of this vessel, developed entirely in Spain, is its propulsion system. "It's an electric boat that solves range problems thanks to a fuel cell powered by an eight-kilogram hydrogen tank. The only byproduct this generates is water vapor," developer Pedro Imaz said. Thanks to this technology, it is capable of travelling an average of 65 nautical miles at twelve knots.

Hydrogen and electricity are also the power source for the truck cab which EH2T and ITA brought to the exhibition. The major innovation of this system is creating a vehicle capable of charging its battery without needing to refuel, while still emitting no pollutants thanks to the hydrogen fuel cell as a generator. Furthermore, this project isn't based on a platform specifically designed for this system; instead, it recycles existing trucks, removing their conventional diesel engines and converting them into electric vehicles.

"The idea behind this truck is for it to be a rolling laboratory where companies can test zero-emission technologies under real-world conditions," one of the project managers said.

The star of this exhibition space was undoubtedly EHang with its EH216-S, an autonomous drone for passenger transport that aims to make this type of mode accessible to as many people as possible. They have already obtained authorisation to operate in China. According to Ehand's Deputy President for Europe and Latin America Ricardo Ortega, they are "collaborating with various civil aviation authorities and regulatory bodies to obtain this certification", with the intention of beginning operations in Europe before 2035.

The eMobility MOW 2026 turned the Palacio de Ferias y Congresos into a fountain of knowledge, with more than 400 innovations from 203 exhibiting companies. Despite differing opinions, there is a common goal: reduce emissions and make the transport of tomorrow more sustainable than that of yesterday.

Special commissioner for reindustrialisation of the Ministry of Industry Jaime David Peris closed the forum by pointing out that "the transition towards decarbonised transport cannot mean a deindustrialisation of the continent", but "a unique opportunity to strengthen industrial bases, technological capacity and strategic autonomy".

He expressed his confidence that Spain can become "one of the European destinations for industrial investment in transport and energy transition", relying on a consolidated industrial base, qualified talent and a firm institutional commitment to projects.

"Sustainable transport must also be possible transport. Environmentally sustainable, yes, but also economically and socially sustainable. We cannot speak of a just transition if part of society is excluded. We cannot move towards climate neutrality by generating economic or territorial fractures. This sector provides millions of jobs and we cannot put them at risk", Sanz de Barros said.

title: Canada's legacy mill sites poised to power new biofuels future
description:
Canada’s legacy mill sites poised to power new biofuels future A new collaboration between Ecostrat Inc. and the BMI Group is set to accelerate Canada’s transition toward advanced biofuels by transforming former pulp and paper mills into next‑generation biorefinery hubs.
At the heart of the initiative is the drive to unlock Canada’s vast fibre resources for renewable energy.
By using BDO Zone Ratings, the partners aim to de‑risk early‑stage development and give investors clearer confidence in the availability, reliability, and sustainability of biomass feedstocks.
Tools like the BDO Zone framework can accelerate similar investments across Canada.
The collaboration signals strong private‑sector support for expanding BDO Zone assessments nationwide, reinforcing Canada’s ambition to become a global leader in sustainable biofuels and the broader bioeconomy.
text:
Canada’s legacy mill sites poised to power new biofuels future

A new collaboration between Ecostrat Inc. and the BMI Group is set to accelerate Canada’s transition toward advanced biofuels by transforming former pulp and paper mills into next‑generation biorefinery hubs.

The partnership will apply BDO Zone Ratings—an investment‑grade system for assessing biomass supply and site readiness—to prepare BMI’s portfolio of legacy industrial sites for large‑scale biofuel production and related biobased industries.

At the heart of the initiative is the drive to unlock Canada’s vast fibre resources for renewable energy. By using BDO Zone Ratings, the partners aim to de‑risk early‑stage development and give investors clearer confidence in the availability, reliability, and sustainability of biomass feedstocks.

This is particularly significant for biofuels developers, who depend on long‑term, stable supply chains to justify major capital investment.

Jordan Solomon, CEO of Ecostrat, noted that Canada has the raw materials and communities eager for new economic opportunities, but often lacks consistent regional intelligence to attract global developers.

BMI Group CEO Paul Veldman emphasised that repurposing former forest‑industry assets for biofuel and bioproduct manufacturing can revitalise rural economies while ensuring every part of the fibre stream is put to productive use.

BMI’s Bioveld North project in Espanola, Ontario, illustrates the model: a full‑fibre bioproduct hub designed to support renewable energy, advanced materials, and next‑generation manufacturing. Tools like the BDO Zone framework can accelerate similar investments across Canada.

The collaboration signals strong private‑sector support for expanding BDO Zone assessments nationwide, reinforcing Canada’s ambition to become a global leader in sustainable biofuels and the broader bioeconomy.

description:

Southern Energy Renewables is a U.S.-based developer of biomass-to-fuels projects.
Register here to be notified about Southern Energy Renewables job opportunities, hiring events and news updates.
About Southern Energy Renewables Inc. Southern Energy Renewables Inc. is a U.S.-based clean fuels, chemicals, and products developer focused on advancing large-scale biomass-to-fuels projects.
SOURCE: Southern Energy Renewables Inc.
text:
The project is expected to create 514 new job opportunities in the Southeast Region.

The flagship project expands U.S. energy innovation to support growing global demand for lower-cost transportation fuels.

Commercial operations are expected to begin in late 2029.

Southern Energy Renewables announced it will invest $1.4 billion to develop a green methanol and sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) production facility that will convert the region's abundant wood-waste biomass into some of the lowest lifecycle-carbon fuels on the market, leveraging Louisiana's established energy infrastructure, innovation ecosystem and skilled workforce.

The company is expected to create 120 direct new jobs with an average salary of $97,267, which is 5% above the average St. Charles Parish wage. Louisiana Economic Development estimates the project will result in an additional 394 indirect new jobs, for a total of 514 potential new job opportunities in the Southeast Region.

"Louisiana's energy leadership is rooted in our ability to couple emerging technologies alongside the industries that have long powered our state," LED Secretary Susan B. Bourgeois said. "Southern Energy Renewables' plans build on that strength by bringing advanced fuel production and high-quality jobs to St. Charles Parish. This project expands the scope of our energy sector and reinforces Louisiana's role in the nation's energy dominance."

The new production facility located near hydrogen supply and key logistics infrastructure is Southern's first commercial-scale development in Louisiana as it prepares for a proposed merger with DevvStream, a carbon management and monetization firm.

"Louisiana is a vital partner in advancing our production model that includes the conversion of regional wood-waste biomass sourcing, fuel production, and aviation and maritime offtake to create a first-of-its-kind platform with the potential to compete on a global stage and reduce the global reliance on China for clean fuels," Southern Energy Renewables CEO Jay Patel said. "With support from LED and other local partners, our roadmap is built to deliver fuels at an industrial scale with a clear cost advantage, while creating new jobs, expanding the local economy, and strengthening America's energy leadership to meet growing demand."

Southern Energy Renewables is a U.S.-based developer of biomass-to-fuels projects. The St. Charles Parish facility represents the company's first commercial-scale development in Louisiana.

"We are excited to welcome Southern Renewable Energy to St. Charles Parish," St. Charles Parish President Matthew Jewell said. "Projects like this continue to demonstrate the industry's confidence in our community, our workforce, and our strategic location along the Mississippi River. This investment will continue to strengthen our economy, support job creation, and ensure our parish remains a leader in alternative fuel and energy production."

Pre-construction planning and site development activities on the project are underway. Construction is expected to begin in late 2027 with production anticipated in late 2029.

"Louisiana has the assets to lead the next era of energy, and Southern Energy Renewables is proof," President and CEO of Greater New Orleans, Inc. Michael Hecht said. "This remarkable project builds on the state's industrial strengths, skilled workforce, and global logistics assets to bring a first-of-its-kind sustainable jet fuel and methanol facility to St. Charles Parish. It will create high-wage jobs while advancing lower-carbon fuels that help modernize the aviation industry."

To win the project in St. Charles, the state of Louisiana offered Southern Energy Renewables a competitive incentives package that includes the comprehensive workforce development solutions of LED FastStart and a $1 million performance-based grant for infrastructure improvements. The company is also expected to participate in the state's Industrial Tax Exemption program.

title: Southern Energy and Axens Sign MoU to Develop Sustainable Aviation Fuel | Intellectia.AI
description:
Southern Energy Renewables and Axens have signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on the development of sustainable aviation fuel and related fuel projects, with an initial focus on Southern's planned biomass-to-fuel facility in Louisiana.
Under the MoU, Southern is responsible for leading overall project development, including site work, permitting, feedstock and offtake engagement, and coordination with local stakeholders.
Axens is slated to serve as licensor-of-record for key technologies across the value chain, including CO2 capture and conditioning and SAF production technologies, with the goal of advancing a de-risked, bankable pathway from regional biomass to low-carbon fuels.
The parties expect to focus first on the Louisiana facility, then evaluate additional opportunities in other regions as appropriate.
In parallel with the Axens collaboration, Southern has created an experimental digital token referred to as "$SAF."
text: Southern Energy Renewables and Axens have signed a memorandum of understanding to collaborate on the development of sustainable aviation fuel and related fuel projects, with an initial focus on Southern's planned biomass-to-fuel facility in Louisiana. Under the MoU, Southern is responsible for leading overall project development, including site work, permitting, feedstock and offtake engagement, and coordination with local stakeholders. Axens is slated to serve as licensor-of-record for key technologies across the value chain, including CO2 capture and conditioning and SAF production technologies, with the goal of advancing a de-risked, bankable pathway from regional biomass to low-carbon fuels. The parties expect to focus first on the Louisiana facility, then evaluate additional opportunities in other regions as appropriate. In parallel with the Axens collaboration, Southern has created an experimental digital token referred to as "$SAF." The current concept is to use $SAF over time as a data layer that helps track and visualize production from Southern's facilities, starting with limited use at the pilot plant and, subject to further development and approvals, extending to the flagship Louisiana project. The framework is intended to link token issuance and retirement to measured output and associated operational data, with the goal of improving traceability around SAF production and providing an additional lens on process performance and optimization, without changing how the underlying fuels are produced, sold, or accounted for.

title: Flight safety statistics: understanding 2025 aviation accidents and safety trends - Airbus
description:
With air traffic reaching an estimated 35.2 million flights in 2025, flying still remains the safest form of travel.
When asking how many aviation accidents per year occur, the data shows that flying remains a highly secure mode of transport.
However, addressing the underlying causes of aviation accidents means technology must be paired with human vigilance and teamwork.
To learn more about safely packing your devices and mitigating the risk of lithium battery fires on aircraft, explore our dedicated article on flying with a lithium battery aircraft device.
Explore the data: 2025 flight safety statistics The goal of any review of aviation accidents is to help the industry further enhance the level of safety.
text:
With air traffic reaching an estimated 35.2 million flights in 2025, flying still remains the safest form of travel. However, evolving threats require unwavering continuous industry-wide vigilance. Explore the latest data and the collaborative actions driving a proactive safety culture across our industry.

When asking how many aviation accidents per year occur, the data shows that flying remains a highly secure mode of transport. In 2025, the commercial aviation network safely transported more than five billion passengers. Air traffic reached an estimated 35.2 million flights, up from almost 34 million in 2024.

The past year recorded six accidents resulting in hull losses, three of which were fatal. This compares to four fatal accidents and twelve hull losses in 2024. While the overall air accident rate remains low, the recurring nature of these events highlights the continued necessity for enhanced safety measures. To manage safety risks effectively, the circumstances of each incident must be meticulously analysed, leading to actionable findings and practical solutions shared across the entire aviation community.

Understanding the causes of aviation accidents

The passenger traffic outlook for 2026 points to a continuing rebound over the next two to three years, trending towards a long-term annual growth rate of around 3.6%. Beyond the correlated increased risk exposure, the aviation industry faces an increasingly complex operational environment, driven by the emergence of new operators, types of operations, and evolving geopolitical threats.

Everyone has a role to play to address these challenges.

However, addressing the underlying causes of aviation accidents means technology must be paired with human vigilance and teamwork.

"Fostering a positive safety culture based on open communication and strong safety leadership is essential to install, grow, and deploy effective safety measures across organisations", says Sophie Rougé, head of Safety Governance at Airbus.

Preventing lithium battery fires on aircraft

With more than five billion passengers transported in 2025, the commercial aviation network carried an estimated 20 to 25 billion Portable Electronic Devices (PEDs). This makes passengers an integral part of the safety equation. The sheer volume of these devices means the risk of lithium battery fires on aircraft is a serious safety threat demanding collective attention.

Regulators (EASA, FAA) and global organisations (ICAO, IATA) continually update their guidance to support all stakeholders. The entire industry must work together to anticipate and mitigate these risks, ensuring every passenger understands their role in keeping everyone safe.

To learn more about safely packing your devices and mitigating the risk of lithium battery fires on aircraft, explore our dedicated article on flying with a lithium battery aircraft device.

Explore the data: 2025 flight safety statistics

The goal of any review of aviation accidents is to help the industry further enhance the level of safety. Sharing safety knowledge, good practices, safety success stories, and lessons learned contributes to safety enhancements across the entire air transport system. Sharing safety information has proven to prevent accidents.

title: Batik Air adjusts fuel surcharges on selected routes amid rising jet fuel prices | The Star
description:
KUALA LUMPUR: Batik Air Malaysia has implemented modest adjustments across selected routes, including fuel surcharges structured by route category, in response to rising operating costs driven by higher jet fuel prices.
"These measures are reviewed regularly in line with market and fuel price developments,” he told Bernama today.
He noted that Batik Air’s airfares are dynamic, reflecting market supply and demand, and they fluctuate according to the competitive environment.
Like many airlines globally, he noted that Batik Air has been closely monitoring the recent surge in jet fuel prices, which have climbed from around US$85-90 per barrel to above US$100 amid geopolitical tensions in West Asia.
Airlines worldwide have been facing increasing cost pressures following the spike in fuel prices, a key component of airline operating costs, as tensions in West Asia continue to affect global energy markets.
text:
KUALA LUMPUR: Batik Air Malaysia has implemented modest adjustments across selected routes, including fuel surcharges structured by route category, in response to rising operating costs driven by higher jet fuel prices.

Chief executive officer Chandran Rama Muthy said the objective was not to fully pass on the cost increases to passengers, but rather to partially offset fuel expenses while remaining competitive and ensuring travellers continue to enjoy fair and flexible pricing.

"These measures are reviewed regularly in line with market and fuel price developments,” he told Bernama today.

He noted that Batik Air’s airfares are dynamic, reflecting market supply and demand, and they fluctuate according to the competitive environment.

"When costs rise, fares may adjust accordingly, and when costs ease, fares follow suit,” he explained.

Like many airlines globally, he noted that Batik Air has been closely monitoring the recent surge in jet fuel prices, which have climbed from around US$85-90 per barrel to above US$100 amid geopolitical tensions in West Asia.

Airlines worldwide have been facing increasing cost pressures following the spike in fuel prices, a key component of airline operating costs, as tensions in West Asia continue to affect global energy markets. - Bernama

outlet: U.S. Senator for Kansas, Jerry Moran
title: Sen. Moran, Colleagues Urge the Administration to Prioritize Farmers in Final 45Z Rule
description:
Still, its success depends on clear, workable rules allowing farmers and biofuel producers to meaningfully participate,” continued the delegation.
We applaud the interagency work among the U.S. Department of Treasury (Treasury), U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), and other agencies in developing this guidance.
Still, its success depends on clear, workable rules allowing farmers and biofuel producers to meaningfully participate—clarity the Biden administration repeatedly failed to provide.
The final rule must not replicate the top-down approach taken by the Biden administration when it implemented a similar program under the section 40B sustainable aviation fuel credit.
Delivering this outcome after years of mismanagement under the previous administration will be a clear win for U.S. farmers and demonstrate the Trump administration’s commitment to a stronger rural America.
text:
WASHINGTON – U.S. Senator Jerry Moran (R-Kan.) – a member of the Senate Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition and Forestry – yesterday joined his colleagues, Sen. Joni Ernst (R-Iowa) and Rep. Mariannette Miller-Meeks (R-IA-01), in urging U.S. Secretary of Treasury Scott Bessent, U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright and U.S. Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins to prioritize farmers, biofuel producers and the agricultural community in the finalized 45Z rule in order to provide clarity, bolster consumer confidence and make certain farmers receive the intended benefits of the tax credit.

“The recent advancement of proposed rulemaking for the Section 45Z clean fuel production credit is a significant and welcome step to deliver long-overdue certainty for farmers, renewable fuel producers, and consumers,” wrote the delegation. “As you finalize the rule, we urge you to ensure farmers can fully benefit from this new market by clearly recognizing and valuing conservation and regenerative practices used in the field.”

“45Z was designed to add value to farmers’ crops and expand domestic production of clean, homegrown biofuels. Still, its success depends on clear, workable rules allowing farmers and biofuel producers to meaningfully participate,” continued the delegation. “Planting season is a few short weeks away in many parts of the country, and farmers need clear answers soon to fully take advantage of 45Z.”

The letter was also signed by Sens. Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa), Deb Fischer (R-Neb.), Michael Rounds (R-S.D.), Roger Marshall (R-Kan.) and Pete Ricketts (R-Neb.) and Reps. Adrian Smith (R-NE-03), Tracey Mann (R-KS-01), Mike Flood (R-NE-01), Michelle Fischbach (R-MN-07), Brad Finstad (R-MN-01), Ashley Hinson (R-IA-02), Zach Nunn (R-IA-03), David Valadao (R-CA-22), Erin Houchin (R-IN-09), Don Bacon (R-NE-02), Darin LaHood (R-IL-16), Derek Schmidt (R-KS-02) and Mike Bost.

In February, Sens. Moran and Ernst introduced the Securing America’s Fuels (SAF) Act to strengthen the sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) industry, provide new markets for farmers and lower emissions in the transportation sector by reinstating the SAF bonus credit that would allow qualifying SAF producers to receive up to $1.75 per gallon, and extending the 45Z tax credit for all clean fuels through 2033.

Full text of the letter can be found here and below.

Dear Secretaries Bessent, Wright, and Rollins,

Thank you for your strong leadership and continued support for America’s biofuel industry. The recent advancement of proposed rulemaking for the Section 45Z clean fuel production credit is a significant and welcome step to deliver long-overdue certainty for farmers, renewable fuel producers, and consumers. We applaud the interagency work among the U.S. Department of Treasury (Treasury), U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), and other agencies in developing this guidance. As you finalize the rule, we urge you to ensure farmers can fully benefit from this new market by clearly recognizing and valuing conservation and regenerative practices used in the field.

45Z was designed to add value to farmers’ crops and expand domestic production of clean, homegrown biofuels. Still, its success depends on clear, workable rules allowing farmers and biofuel producers to meaningfully participate—clarity the Biden administration repeatedly failed to provide. Indeed, Treasury under the previous administration did not issue an “intent to propose rulemaking” until January 10, 2025, after the credit had already taken effect. The lack of urgency sent a shock to the farm economy in our states.

To make matters worse, the updated Greenhouse gases, Regulated Emissions, and Energy use in Technologies (GREET) model published by DOE under Biden came five days later and omitted USDA’s farm calculator entirely. In practice, this omission effectively excluded the American farmer from the model used to determine credit values.

Thankfully, at the onset of President Trump’s return to the White House, and with the positive comments from each of you in your testimonies before Congress, it was clear rural America would no longer be on the back burner, and unleashing homegrown biofuels would be a priority. Your early and continued actions signal a decisive shift towards putting farmers and rural communities first. Building on this momentum, we proudly secured many necessary modifications in the One Big Beautiful Bill (OBBB). These wins include extending the credit through 2029, removing unfair environmental penalties against U.S. farmers, promoting the use of American-grown crops, and reversing the political modeling bias, all of which better position farmers and rural businesses to utilize 45Z.

The newly released proposed rulemaking from Treasury on February 4, 2026, in coordination with USDA, DOE, and other agencies, appropriately moves 45Z towards its final form and creates new domestic demand for the American farmer—aligning with the President’s priority to create more markets here at home for U.S. producers. As this rule is finalized, it is critical the final rule clearly identifies the types of on-farm conservation practices capable of generating lower-carbon feedstocks and ultimately qualifying for a premium under 45Z. Examples of these practices include, but are not limited to no-till, cover crops, strip till, manure application, and optimized fertilizer use.

The final rule must not replicate the top-down approach taken by the Biden administration when it implemented a similar program under the section 40B sustainable aviation fuel credit. Those rules required farmers to “bundle” a select few practices regardless of the facts on the ground. Instead, 45Z guidance should provide farmers with maximum flexibility to implement a wide range of practices based on what works best for their farm. Moreover, the guidance must ensure the credit does not box out family farmers from participating by imposing unnecessary red tape or overly prescriptive requirements.

We are thankful for commitments within the proposed rulemaking affirming Treasury will soon include the new 45ZCF FD-CIC to account for carbon intensity reductions attributed to agricultural practices. While this is encouraging, we strongly urge that the new feedstock calculator be fully integrated into the 45ZCF GREET model in a timely manner. Only with this integration can farmers’ real-world conservation efforts and regenerative practices be accurately measured and rewarded under 45Z. Accordingly, we respectfully request this integration be finalized and published expeditiously.

Consistent with the OBBB’s mandate to reward agricultural innovation, we also ask for the swift implementation of distinct emission pathways for animal manure-derived renewable natural gas (RNG). Providing specific rates for dairy and swine manure feedstocks within the 45ZCF GREET model is essential to turning traditional liabilities into profit centers for our livestock producers.

In addition, we want to stress that farmer participation under 45Z should be structured using a “Book and Claim” accounting framework. This approach ensures farmers can generate value from lower-carbon practices without requiring the physical delivery of grain to a specific biofuel facility. A mass balance system, by contrast, would force acres to compete and create unnecessary challenges for farmers located far from biofuel facilities or those who rely on grain for livestock feed. “Book and Claim” avoids these distortions, broadens participation, and allows 45Z to function as a truly nationwide market opportunity for American producers.

Planting season is a few short weeks away in many parts of the country, and farmers need clear answers soon to fully take advantage of 45Z. Timely inclusion of the 45ZCF FD-CIC, updated pathways and farm practices in the updated 45ZCF GREET model, and the use of “Book and Claim” accounting will provide the necessary clarity, create confidence in the marketplace, and ensure farmers can actually receive a premium for their farming efforts. Delivering this outcome after years of mismanagement under the previous administration will be a clear win for U.S. farmers and demonstrate the Trump administration’s commitment to a stronger rural America.

Again, we thank you for your work to put the American farmer and biofuel producer first. Thank you for your attention to this important matter.

# # #

title: Flames shoot into sky after fire at Pasadena chemical plant - BIC Magazine
description:
A fire broke out at a LyondellBasell chemical plant near Pasadena, Texas, on Thursday night, sending thick smoke and flames into the air and prompting a large emergency response from multiple agencies.
The fire was reported around 9 p.m. at LyondellBasell's Bayport Choate site, a sprawling chemical complex located at 10801 Choate Road in Pasadena, 20 miles east of Houston.
Officials said the fire burned for several hours overnight, with flames and thick smoke visible from miles away.
As of early Friday morning, officials said the fire was mostly out, though first responders and industrial response crews remained at the site monitoring the scene.
Once the fire is fully extinguished and the scene is safe, the Harris County Fire Marshal’s Office is expected to begin an investigation.
text:
A fire broke out at a LyondellBasell chemical plant near Pasadena, Texas, on Thursday night, sending thick smoke and flames into the air and prompting a large emergency response from multiple agencies.

No injuries were reported.

The fire was reported around 9 p.m. at LyondellBasell's Bayport Choate site, a sprawling chemical complex located at 10801 Choate Road in Pasadena, 20 miles east of Houston. The Harris County Fire Marshal's Office identified the facility as a Chemical Process Unit at Lyondell BayPort and confirmed that everyone present at the time of the fire was accounted for.

Officials said the fire burned for several hours overnight, with flames and thick smoke visible from miles away. As of early Friday morning, officials said the fire was mostly out, though first responders and industrial response crews remained at the site monitoring the scene.

The cause of the fire is not yet known. Once the fire is fully extinguished and the scene is safe, the Harris County Fire Marshal’s Office is expected to begin an investigation.

More on this story at KHOU.

text:
It will come as no surprise that the volatility of oil prices has reached the aviation fuel market and costs are going up. Although media coverage of the increases is as volatile as the market itself, the price of Jet A appears to have briefly shot up more than 80% late last week before settling around 50% higher than on Feb. 27, the day before Israel and the U.S. attacked Iran. The jet fuel prices have roughly followed crude oil prices, which are now about 40% higher than before the attacks began. Jet fuel is more susceptible to shortages because it must be kept separate from other forms of kerosene, so supply issues in coming weeks are anticipated. Airfares have already started increasing.

As for 100LL, the price has been less volatile and is ranging between about $4 to $6 a gallon in most areas, with an average of $5.98 according to AirNav.com. It says the average price of Jet A is $6.07 and mogas is at $4.91 a gallon. Global Air says prices are higher than that. Its map says the national average for 100LL is $6.50 a gallon and Jet A is at $6.85. Sustainable aviation fuel is a whopping $9.31 on average.
